name: Patrol
description: An AI behavior where an agent follows a route, area, or schedule while scanning for stimuli.
design_notes: Patrols create predictable enemy presence and readable risk. The route is only one part of the mechanic; look direction, pauses, hearing, alert transitions, and recovery to route all determine how players plan around it.
design_purpose: Give players observable patterns they can learn, avoid, exploit, or interrupt.
Populate spaces with believable activity before combat or alarm states begin.
Support stealth, horror, and strategy pacing through controlled enemy coverage.
player_fantasy: The player feels clever when they read a patrol pattern and move through danger at the right moment.

parameters.name: waypoint_count
parameters.description: Number of route points used by the patrol.

parameters.name: pause_at_waypoint_ms
parameters.description: Dwell window before moving to the next route point.

parameters.name: look_sweep_angle_degrees
parameters.description: Optional scan arc used while pausing or walking.

parameters.name: route_resume_delay_ms
parameters.description: Delay before returning to patrol after investigation or chase.

parameters.name: patrol_variation_seed
parameters.description: Seed for route variation when deterministic randomness is desired.

edge_cases: Blocked navigation should choose wait, reroute, teleport fallback, or alert transition.
After chasing or investigating, the AI needs a clear rule for resuming at nearest, next, or original waypoint.
Multiple AI sharing a route should avoid overlapping bodies and identical synchronized pauses unless intentional.
Save/load mid-route must restore path target, look direction, and perception state.
common_bugs: AI rotates instantly at waypoints and unfairly spots the player during a visual pop.
Route index advances even when navigation fails, causing the AI to skip important coverage.
Perception keeps running during scripted pauses that designers expected to be safe.
Patrol state never resumes after last-known-position search because the investigation flag is not cleared.
balancing_notes: Long predictable routes favor stealth planning; random routes create tension but can feel unfair without readable cues.
Patrol coverage should leave intentional gaps, tools, or timing windows for the player.
accessibility_notes: Provide readable enemy facing, footstep, or suspicion cues so patrol timing is not audio-only.
Avoid requiring players to memorize long invisible schedules without environmental landmarks.

implementation_notes.unity: Use NavMeshAgent or custom grid navigation with route data stored separately from perception; expose gizmos for waypoint order and look arcs.
implementation_notes.godot: Use NavigationAgent2D or NavigationAgent3D with a behavior-state script that stores current waypoint, pause timer, and perception state.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use Behavior Trees or StateTree with Blackboard keys for route index, investigation target, and resume policy; keep perception events separate from movement tasks.
implementation_notes.web: Represent routes as graph nodes and update AI decisions on a fixed tick; keep pathfinding, perception, and animation state independent.
